segments.s705-g81: همه بار کردند is a past-tense clause: همه (an indefinite/quantifying pronoun/adverb 'all/everything') is the object of بار کردن ('to load'), with کردند as the 3rd-person plural past of کردن used as the light verb. و is the coordinating conjunction linking the next clause. خود is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun ('himself') placed before the verb to stress that the subject acts personally. برنشست is a literary past form composed of the preverb بر- + نشستن (نشست, 3rd-singular past), meaning 'he mounted/took his seat' — note the shift from کردَند (they loaded) to برنشست (he sat/mounted) where خود marks the singular principal actor.
segments.s705-g82: میان is an adverb/prepositional adverb meaning 'in the middle' or 'amidst' and here sets the scene for the action that follows. از پی بازگشتن is an infinitival/prepositional phrase: از پی ('after/in pursuit of') + بازگشتن ('to return'), functioning as the thing affected — i.e. 'the way/possibility of returning' or 'pursuit of return.' ببست is a literary past 3rd-singular of بستن (here with an extra initial ب- typical in epic diction) meaning 'he closed/blocked.' Together the clause says that, positioned in the middle, he blocked/closed off the route of return (i.e. prevented pursuit/retreat).
segments.s705-g41: همه (subject) + پاک (adjective) = “all are pure.” در introduces a locative/relational phrase here (sense “in/within”), and زینهار is an older noun meaning “warning/safeguard.” منند is the poetic/copulative verb (like مانَد) meaning “are like.” Grammatically the clause gives the first simile: “all are pure, like a zinehār/safeguard.”
segments.s705-g42: به marks the comparison/relationship (“as/to”), تاج اندرون is a reversed genitival phrase for “the crown’s inner part” (اندرون = inside/within), گوشوار is an ornament/earring, and منند again gives the simile “are like.” The whole segment says they serve as inner ornaments (ear‑jewels) to the crown.
segments.s705-g43: برآمد is a past-tense verb “(it) rose/appeared.” هم here is an emphatic/adverbial “also/too,” آنگه is a temporal adverb “then,” and شب is the subject “night.” از + تیره کوه is a prepositional phrase “from the dark mountain” where تیره modifies کوه. Word order is poetic but the sense is “then the night rose from the dark mountain.”
segments.s705-g44: سپه (“army/host”) is the topical subject and بازگشتند is the 3rd-person plural past “they returned.” هر دو گروه is a quantifying phrase “both groups/companies” specifying who returned; placed after the verb for poetic inversion. The sense: “both companies of the army returned.”
segments.s705-g45: چوآمد is a temporal clause introducer = “when (it) came.” غوپاسبان (cowherds/herdsmen) and جرس (the bell or the ringing) follow as the things that arrived/occurred. The clause sets the time/context for what follows: “when the herdsmen and the bell came…”
segments.s705-g46: ز (from) + لشکر (the host/army) marks the domain. نبد is an archaic negative past form (≈ نبود) “there was not / there were not.” خفته is a past participle “asleep,” and بسیار کس is “many people.” Word order is inverted: the clause states that among the army there were not many asleep.
segments.s705-g47: جهان جوی is a compound noun (جهان + جوی) meaning “one who seeks the world” or “world‑seeker” (جوی here = searcher). بندوی is an epithet/appositive “bondman/servant.” ز آنجا برفت is a simple past clause “he went away from there” (ز = from, آنجا = there, برفت = archaic past of رفتن ‘he went’). The whole unit names the person and says he departed from that place.
segments.s705-g48: میان دو لشکر = “between the two armies” (میان = between, دو لشکر = two hosts). خرامید is a past verb meaning “he strode/pranced proudly.” تفت here appears postposed and functions as the proper name of the person who strode (word order inversion is poetic). The final sense: he proudly strode between the two armies. (Because the supplied lexical senses for تفت do not match a proper‑name reading, its sense index is set to -1.)
segments.s705-g49: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/at' marking the target of the look; لشکر is the noun 'the army/host' as the object of that preposition. نگه here is the noun 'gaze' used with the light verb کرد (نگه کردن) to form the verb phrase 'to look'; کرد is simple past (he looked). کنداوری is a proper name in apposition (the doer of the action). The phrase therefore means 'Kandauri looked at the army.'
segments.s705-g50: This is a descriptive nominal phrase in apposition to the name: خوش‌آواز ('melodious') + و ('and') + گویا used here as 'eloquent' (not 'it seems') modifying منادیگری ('heralding' or 'the office/act of a herald'). Grammatically it describes Kandauri's manner — 'a melodious and eloquent herald.'
segments.s705-g51: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he said' / 'he commanded' (used here as 'ordered'). تا introduces a subordinate clause with a purposive/result sense 'so that' or 'that'; بارگی is the clause's subject (here a proper name Bargī — not well represented by the provided noun senses), and برنشست is the past form 'sat up' / 'took a seat.' The clause thus reads 'he ordered that Bargī sit up.'
segments.s705-g52: به here marks the means ('by' / 'with'), turning the following verbal noun into an adverbial: بیدار کردن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'rousing/waking (up)' with کردن as the light verb. میان را is the definite object 'the middle/gap' (را is the direct-object marker), and ببست is the past tense 'he shut/closed' (archaic/poetic prefixed form). The whole clause means 'by rousing (them) he closed the gap.'
segments.s705-g53: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' setting the manner. تا again introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). میان 'the space between' and دولشکر 'the two hosts' form the object of the verb. براند is a classical/present (subjunctive/jussive-like) form of راندن 'to drive/ride (in)', here expressing the desired/resultant action 'that he may drive (into)'.
segments.s705-g54: کزو is a compound prepositional pronoun 'from which / from whom' referring back to that action/space; تا once more marks purpose/result 'so that.' بدشمن is an adjective-noun 'hostile/bad enemy' used generically for enemy forces; فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'in great number' and نماند is the negative past/subjunctive-like 'remain / be left' (here 'no longer remain' as the intended outcome). The clause means 'from which no large enemy force would remain.'
segments.s705-g55: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/clamor' and برآورد here is a verbal compound meaning 'he raised/uttered' (برآورد کردن = to bring forth). کای is the vocative particle 'O' (addressing people), and بندگان is the addressee 'servants/people/subjects' used as a general vocative. Together: 'He raised a cry, "O people..."'.
segments.s705-g56: گنه کرده is a participial phrase ('having sinned' / 'sinners') where گنه is 'sin' and کرده is the perfect participle 'done' making a descriptive label. و is the conjunction 'and.' بخت جویندگان is a nominal phrase 'seekers of fortune' (بخت 'fortune/luck' + جویندگان 'seekers'). The vocative thus lists those being addressed: 'sinners and seekers of fortune.'
segments.s705-g33: یکی لشکرست is a compressed poetic order meaning ‘(it) is one/one single host’; لشکرست is the contraction of لشکر است (is an army/host). این چومور وملخ is the nominative/topic placed after that predicate: این (this) modifies the noun phrase چومور و ملخ (mud and locusts) which functions as the logical subject of لشکرست.
segments.s705-g34: گرفته is a past participle/verb meaning ‘has taken/seized’ and takes بیابان as its object; the order is verb + object (poetic). همه ریگ و شخ is a coordinated noun phrase (‘all the sand and shakh’), where همه ‘all/entire’ quantifies the joined nouns ریگ (sand) and شخ (an onomatopoeic/indeterminate word for small stones or dust).
segments.s705-g35: نه introduces the first member of a neither…nor construction. والا بود is the past copula construction ‘was noble/exalted’ (بود = was). خیره is an adverbial/participial modifier ‘staring/gaping’, and خون ریختن is an infinitival/verbal-noun phrase ‘to shed blood’ functioning as the complement of والا بود (i.e. ‘to gape and shed blood was not noble’).
segments.s705-g36: نه continues the negative pairing (‘nor’). این شاه is the noun phrase subject ‘this king’. با بنده آویختن is an infinitival phrase ‘to cling to a servant’ (با marks the object/target and آویختن is the infinitive ‘to cling/attach oneself’), giving the second thing judged unseemly.
segments.s705-g37: هر آنکس که is the indefinite relative ‘whoever/anyone who’. خواهد is present/future ‘wants/desires’. ز ما marks the source ‘from us’, and زینهار is the noun ‘a warning/admonition’ that the person desires; the whole chunk means ‘whoever wishes a warning from us’.
segments.s705-g38: به از is a poetic comparative construction meaning ‘better than’. کشته (past participle) and خسته (adjective) are the two compared states ‘killed/slain’ or ‘weary/exhausted’, and در کارزار (‘in battle’) locates these states—i.e. ‘better than being killed or exhausted in the battlefield.’
segments.s705-g39: بدو گفت خسرو is inverted poetic word order: بدو ‘to him’ + گفت ‘said’ + خسرو (the speaker) — ‘Khosrow said to him’. که introduces the content clause. هر کز گناه is the relative phrase ‘whoever who has sinned/whose sin (it is)’ (کز = that/who; گناه = sin/guilt).
segments.s705-g40: بپیچید and برو are imperatives/commands (‘turn aside!’ / ‘go!’) directed at the addressee(s). من نیم is an archaic negated copula meaning ‘I am not’ (نیم = contracted/poetic form of نیستم), and کینه خواه is a compound noun ‘one who seeks grudge/vengeance’—together: ‘I am not a grudge-seeker.’
segments.s705-g17: سبک here is an adjective meaning 'light' (of weight or movement) modifying دمان 'hem/skirt'; the phrase is arranged with the modifier before the possessed noun phrase ('سبکِ دَمانِ شهریار' compressed into 'سبک شهریار دمان'), and اندر آمد is a verb phrase 'entered/came in' placed after the noun phrase, a common poetic word order.
segments.s705-g18: به marks the indirect object 'to' or 'toward'; بهرام چوبینه functions as a proper name with an epithet (Bahram Chubin — 'Chubin' = club-bearer), and بد نشان is a noun phrase 'ill-omened sign' where بد (prepositive) modifies نشان. The main verb ('نشان داد' or similar) is omitted by poetic ellipsis and must be supplied from context ('he made/gave/ showed an ill-omened sign to Bahram Chubin').
segments.s705-g19: بزد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb 'he struck'; نیزه‌ای is an indefinite direct object 'a spear' (the -ای suffix marks indefiniteness); بر + کمربند اوی is a prepositional phrase 'against/on his belt' with اوی as the archaic 3rd-person possessive ('his'). Word order places the verb first (poetic) followed by object and then the prepositional phrase.
segments.s705-g20: زره بود uses بود as a past copula/emphatic 'there was/was on (him) armor'; نگسست is best read as negative past ('ن' + گسست) meaning 'did not break' (the supplied sense entry gives 'break'); پیوند اوی = 'its fastening/strap' with اوی again the possessive 'his'. Together the clause means 'his armor held; its fastenings did not break.'
segments.s705-g21: سنان 'spear-tip' is the noun subject; سر نیزه شد literally 'became the head of the spear' but here means the tip was affected (i.e., fractured), and به دونیم should be read as 'into two halves' (poetic fusion of دو نیم). شد is the past 'became/was' and marks the resulting state. Note that دونیم in this line functions as an adverbial phrase 'into two'.
segments.s705-g22: دلِ مرد is a genitival/possessive phrase 'the man's heart'; بی‌راه شد is an idiomatic predication 'became astray/went awry' (i.e., the heart was thrown off its course), and پر ز بیم is a descriptive phrase 'full of fear' with پر functioning as 'full (of)' and ز marking the cause/source ('with/from fear'). Word order places the predicate after the subject in this poetic line.
segments.s705-g23: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'; بشکست is past 'it broke' (3rd singular); نیزه is the subject 'the spear'; بر here is the preposition 'against' or simply linking the action to the object; آشفت شاه places the stative verb آشفت 'was upset/agitated' before the subject شاه 'the king' (inverted poetic order), so 'the king was agitated when the spear broke.'
segments.s705-g24: بزد is past 'he struck'; تیغ is the direct object 'sword'; بر + مغفر indicates the target 'at the maghfar' (maghfar here a piece of covering/armor or a covering part); کینه خواه is a postpositive descriptive compound 'revenge-seeking' (کینه 'hatred/grudge' + خواه 'seeking/wanting'), qualifying the action or actor — 'struck ... in a spirit of vengeance.'
segments.s705-g57: هران کز شما او گنهکارتر is a relative clause starting with هران (an archaic/plural demonstrative meaning ‘each of them’ or ‘those’), followed by the relativizer کز (here = “who/that”) and the group شما او گنهکارتر (‘among you than he guiltier’). Word order is inverted/elliptical: هران کز شما = “whoever of you,” and گنهکارتر is the comparative adjective ‘more sinful/guiltier’ modifying the understood subject.
segments.s705-g58: به جنگ اندرون نامبردارتر continues the sentence with a prepositional phrase and a comparative predicate. به here gives a locative/goal sense (‘in/at’), جنگ اندرون = ‘inner battle/war’ (جنگ + اندرون ‘within’), and نامبردارتر is the comparative form of نامبردار (‘renowned’), i.e. ‘the more renowned.’ The comparative links back to گنهکارتر: ‘the guiltier (he) the more renowned in inner combat.’
segments.s705-g59: به یزدانش بخشید شاه جهان places the verb بخشید (past) with its indirect object and subject in a compact, poetic order. به یزدانش = ‘to/unto his God’ (an indirect object), بخشید here carries the sense of seeking/receiving pardon (see note) or ‘asked to be forgiven,’ and شاه جهان (‘the king of the world’) is the grammatical subject. Poetic word order puts the verb before the subject.
segments.s705-g60: گناهی‌که کرد آشکار و نهان is a relative clause specifying the thing for which pardon was sought: گناهی‌که = ‘the sin that…’ with the enclitic ـِـکه attaching the relative; کرد is the past of کردن ‘to do’ (he had done); آشکار و نهان are a paired antithesis ‘open/manifest and hidden/secret,’ describing both public and private sins.
segments.s705-g61: به تیره شبان چون برآمد خروش is a temporal clause. به تیره شبان = ‘on/at the dark nights’ (به marking the temporal/location phrase), چون here functions as a temporal/conditional conjunction ‘when/when suddenly,’ برآمد is the past ‘arose/issued forth,’ and خروش = ‘a cry/clamor.’ Word order is poetic: temporal phrase + conjunction + verb + noun.
segments.s705-g62: نهادند هرکس به آواز گوش contains the finite verb نهادند (past, 3rd pl. of نهادن ‘to place/set’), followed by the subject هرکس ‘each person/everyone’ (typical V–S inversion in poetry), and the prepositional phrase به آواز گوش ‘to the voice/for the ear’ meaning ‘they bent their ear to the sound’ (گوش here = attention/ear). The whole means each person set/bent (his) ear toward the voice.
segments.s705-g63: همه نامداران بهرامیان is a straightforward noun phrase: همه ‘all/every,’ نامداران ‘the renowned/illustrious ones,’ and بهرامیان specifies their affiliation ‘the Bahramians’ (followers/retainers of Bahram). The three words form a plural subject meaning ‘all the famed Bahramian men.’
segments.s705-g64: برفتن ببستند یک سر میان is a compact, idiomatic sequence. برفتن is an infinitival/nominal verbal idea ‘to go/for departing,’ and ببستند (past, 3rd pl.) here carries the sense ‘they bound/tied/girded’ (بستن used of tying a headcloth or girding up). یک سر میان literally ‘one head in the midst’ is an adverbial/locative phrase meaning roughly ‘together/as one’ or ‘with one accord.’ The sense is: when preparing to depart, they girded (their) heads and went unitedly.
segments.s705-g25: سراسر همه (adverbial phrase) puts the scene-wide scope up front: “throughout / all around.” تیغ is the noun “sword(s).” برهم here is an adverbial/qualifier meaning “against one another / in mutual collision,” and شکست is the past tense of شکستن “to break” (3rd person singular) — together this yields “swords clashed/broke everywhere.” Word order is poetic: scene adverbial + noun phrase + verb.
segments.s705-g26: بدان is a prepositional phrase (بِ + آن) meaning “to/on that”; پیکر is the noun “body” and is the locus. مغفر is the covering/veil/face‑covering that functions as the subject that follows; اندر is a locative adverb “in/within/on,” and نشست is the past tense verb “sat / settled” (3rd person singular). The clause literally reads “on that body the covering settled,” with the locative and subject appearing before the verb in typical Persian poetic order.
segments.s705-g27: همی is a durative adverb meaning “continually / kept on.” آفرین is an acclamation/interjection “bravo / praise” and کرد is the past tense of کردن “to do / to make” — here ‘کرد’ takes آفرین as its object: “gave praise / cried ‘bravo’.” هرکس که دید is a relative clause “whoever saw,” functioning as the (implicit) subject; Persian poetry often places the verb before its subject clause.
segments.s705-g28: هم is a conjunction/adverb “also / likewise.” آنکس که introduces another relative clause “that person who…,” followed by آواز “the sound/voice” and آهن used poetically for iron/steel (the clang of weapons). شنید is the past tense verb “heard.” The clause links to the previous with هم and parallels its structure.
segments.s705-g29: گرانمایگان is a plural noun meaning “the illustrious/weighty (noble) ones.” از پس اندر is a compound locational/temporal phrase “from the rear / after (coming) in,” where از پس = “from behind/after” and اندر = “into / within.” شدند is the past plural copular/intransitive verb (they went/entered / they became), so the whole means “the nobles went in from the rear.”
segments.s705-g30: چنان is an adverb “thus / in such a way.” لشکری را marks “an army” as a definite object (را). بهم here conveys the result “into disorder / together” and بر زدند is the past plural of زدن with بر as a prefixed/auxiliary preposition meaning “to strike/attack / put to flight.” So the verb phrase چنان ... بر زدند means “so they struck that army, throwing it into disarray.”
segments.s705-g31: خرامید is the past tense verb “he strode / swaggered” (3rd person singular). بندوی is the subject “the bondman / servant (or devotee)” and نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase “near the king.” The verb precedes its subject here as a poetic inversion: “the bondman strode proudly near the king.”
segments.s705-g32: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory particle (here: addressing or introducing speech). تاج تو is the noun phrase “your crown,” followed by the comparative برتر از “higher / superior to” with از introducing the standard of comparison. چرخ ماه is a poetic pairing (the celestial “wheel/heaven” and the moon) serving as the comparator. The clause reads as direct address: “O — your crown is higher than the heavens/the moon.”
segments.s705-g1: هم آنگه is a temporal/affirmative phrase (= 'then' / 'right then'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; کوه is its object 'mountain'; اندر آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'came forth/descended' (اندر = in/into + آمد = came). In this poetic order the prepositional phrase (ز کوه) precedes the verb and the noun سپاه (the army) appears after آمد as the acted-upon/subject-like noun.
segments.s705-g2: جهان شد is a copular/result clause 'the world became' (شد = became). The phrase ز گرد سواران سیاه gives the cause/quality: ز = 'from/of', گرد here is the noun 'dust' (not the verbal/adverbial sense), سواران سیاه = 'black horsemen'. Together: 'the world became (filled) from the dust of the black horsemen.'
segments.s705-g3: وزان is a connective/adverbial 'and/thereupon/from that'; روی functions adverbially here as 'on/at (that occasion)'; بهرام is the proper-name subject; لشکر (army) is the object/thing acted on; براند is the verb 'drove/put to flight/put in motion' (classical narrative form used like a past or historical present). The clause means roughly 'thereupon Bahram drove (his) army.'
segments.s705-g4: به‌روز literally 'by day' or 'in daylight'; اندرون = 'inside/within'; روشنایی = 'light, brightness'; نماند is the past-negative sense 'did not remain / there remained no.' Word order places the time adverb first and the negated verb last: 'by day there remained no light inside.'
segments.s705-g5: همی‌گفت is the iterative/habitual past 'kept saying' (a classical narrative marker meaning 'they used to say' or 'people would say'). هرکس که راند سپاه is a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who drove an army' where راند is the past verb 'drove/expelled' and سپاه is its object.
segments.s705-g6: This is a nominal predicate with the modal بایَد: خرد باید = 'wisdom is necessary' (باید here is impersonal 'it is necessary'); و links the coordinated nouns. مردی here means 'manliness/courage' (a quality), and دستگاه refers to 'system/organization' or proper command/establishment. Together: 'wisdom, manliness, and organization are required.'
segments.s705-g7: دلیران = 'the brave (men)'. که دیدند is a relative/temporal clause 'who/when they saw'. خشت مرا = 'my brick' or 'my building-block' where مرا is the object pronoun (written here split as م + را: م = clitic referring to 'me/my' and را = object marker). Literally: 'the brave, when they saw my brick…'.
segments.s705-g8: همان = 'the same/that very'; پهلوانی = 'heroism/feats of a champion'; سرشت here is a verb meaning 'created/formed' (classical perfective/past sense of سرشتن); مرا (again split m + را) is the object 'me'. The clause says that that very heroism 'formed/made' the speaker (i.e. made him into a hero).
segments.s705-g9: مرا is the accusative 'me' (here written as م + را); Persian often places the object before the verb in poetic/archaic lines. برگزیدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they chose/selected'. بر before خسروان marks the relation 'over/above' (literally 'on kings'), and خسروان is the plural 'kings'. The clause therefore means 'they chose me over the kings.'
segments.s705-g10: به خاک + افگنم: به خاک 'to/into the earth' and افگنم is an older/poetic first‑person form (optative/subjunctive) related to افکندن 'to cast/throw', meaning 'that I be cast' or 'I may cast'; thus 'to cast (me/my name) into the earth' or 'to be cast into the earth'. نام نوشین روان is a noun phrase: نام 'name', نوشین 'sweet/delightful', روان here has the adjectival/participial sense 'flowing/animated' (a common poetic use), so 'sweet, flowing (or living) name.' Word order places the goal phrase before the noun phrase describing the 'name.'
segments.s705-g11: ز لشکر 'from the army/host' uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from/of'. بر شاه شد is literally 'fell upon the king / the king became' with شد as past 'became' and بر indicating 'upon/on the king'. خیره is an adjective 'staring/astonished' describing the king. خیر here is a short exclamatory/nominal element (in older usage often rendering a sense of 'good/strange' or an exclamation); together the phrase conveys that the king was much astonished.
segments.s705-g12: کمان را marks 'the bow' as a definite object (را). The verb complex بزه کرد is irregular/poetic here: syntactically it is X را ... کرد 'he did X to the bow' (کرد is the light verb 'did/made'), so the sense is that he took hold of/strung/drew the bow (an action performed on the bow). و یک چوبه تیر 'and a single shaft (of arrow)' is a noun phrase appended by coordination; چوبه تیر = 'arrow‑shaft' or 'a shaft of an arrow' (he readies an arrow).
segments.s705-g13: بزد ناگهان is verb + adverb: بزد (past 'he struck/smote') and ناگهان 'suddenly'. بر کمرگاه شاه is a locative phrase 'upon/at the king's waist' (بر = on/upon, کمرگاه = waist/loins, شاه = king), so the clause means 'he suddenly struck at the king's waist.'
segments.s705-g14: بکژ is a problematic/archaic item here (possibly a name or an interjection) and is marked uncertain in the lexicon; the main clause is اندر آویخت پیکان به راه: اندر 'in/within' (locative), آویخت past 'hung' or 'was suspended/was fixed', پیکان 'the arrow', به راه 'in/on the way/path' — idiomatically 'the arrow stuck (and) hung in the path/was fixed there.' Grammatically this is a locative/resultative clause describing where the arrow ended up.
segments.s705-g15: یکی بنده is 'a certain servant' (یکی = 'one, a certain', بنده = 'servant'). چون here functions temporally 'when' (common in older Persian: چون + past = 'when/after'), introducing the subordinate clause زخم پیکان بدید 'he saw the arrow‑wound' (زخم = wound, پیکان = arrow, بدید = past 'he saw').
segments.s705-g16: بیامد 'he came' (simple past). ز دیباش بیرون is a locative phrase 'out from the di'bā (brocade/drapery)' with ز = 'from' and بیرون = 'out'. کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to pull/draw' and here means 'he pulled (it) out' — grammatically the verb takes an understood object (the arrow) and completes the action begun in the previous clause.
segments.s705-g73: به یاران = a dative prepositional phrase 'to his companions' placed before the verb (normal in Persian). چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین 'thus' + گفت past of گفتن). کاکنون is the fused/kā+aknun (که اکنون) meaning 'that now' or 'that at present' and introduces what follows; گریز (a noun) is the subject/topic here ('flight'/'fleeing') placed before the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s705-g74: به آید = literary third‑person present/future of آمدن 'it comes/ will come' (here referring back to گریز). ز آرام = 'from (the) calm/quiet' (ز is the poetic 'from'). با رستخیز = 'with an uprising' (با = 'with'; رستخیز a literary noun meaning 'uprising/resurrection'). The phrase assembles as 'the flight will come from peace with an uprising.'
segments.s705-g75: شتر (camel) is the direct object; خواست is the past tense of خواستن ('he wanted/asked for') and takes the source marked by از ساروان ('from the caravan‑leaders'). The numeral phrase 'سه هزار' quantifies the object; in prose/poetry numerals may follow the verb or noun.
segments.s705-g76: هیونان is a plural noun 'beasts/animals' applied to the camels. کفک افگن is an adjectival epithet: کفک ('little foam' here) + افگن (causative/'producing' or 'making') — together a poetic description 'foam‑spraying' or 'foam‑flecked'. و نامدار = 'and renowned' (two coordinated epithets).
segments.s705-g77: ز introduces a partitive/listing 'from'. چیزی 'anything/something' is followed by the relative clause introduced by که 'that/which'. در گنج 'in the treasury' locates the items. بردنی is a participial adjective 'to be carried/portable'; here the clause means 'anything in the treasury that is portable / that can be taken.'
segments.s705-g78: ز گستردنیها = 'from the گستردنی‑things' (a plural nominalized adjective meaning things meant to be spread out — e.g., cloths, carpets, spreads). و از خوردنی = 'and from the edibles/food.' Note the parallel use of ز and از as 'from' in a list.
segments.s705-g79: ز introduces another list 'from'. زرین و سیمین are adjectives used nominally: 'golden (things) and silvery (things)'. وز here is the orthographic cluster representing 'و ز' (and/from) before تخت عاج 'an ivory throne' (تخت 'throne' + عاج 'ivory'). The sequence lists precious objects taken from the treasury.
segments.s705-g80: همان 'that same' or 'likewise' resumes items already mentioned. یاره in this inventory context names an article/ornament (the exact nuance is archaic/poetic and not 'companion' here). و طوق زرین وتاج = 'and a golden collar (necklace) and (a) crown' (طوق 'collar/necklace', زرین 'golden', وتاج 'crown').
segments.s705-g65: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past-time clause. برزد is an archaic past verb (third‑person singular) meaning 'came/appeared/rose' (prefix بر- + root), and here it governs the following noun سر 'summit/top'. از + کوه gives the origin 'from the mountain'. گیتی فروز is a postpositive epithet: گیتی 'world/earth' modified by فروز 'bright', placed after the noun in poetic word order.
segments.s705-g66: زمین را is topicalized by the object marker را ('the earth' as definite/topic). به + ملحم here expresses instrument/means ('with/by carnage'); بیاراست is a past transitive verb 'adorned/arrayed' (third person), and روز at the end is the grammatical subject, personifying 'the day' — typical poetic postposition of the subject after its predicate.
segments.s705-g67: همه دشت is a nominal phrase 'the whole/plain entire plain' (همه = whole/entire). بی‌مرد functions as a descriptive compound meaning 'without men/deserted' modifying دشت; خرگاه 'donkey‑stall/lowly lodging' is conjoined with و. بود is the past copula 'was', completing the predicate 'was deserted and stallless'.
segments.s705-g68: که introduces a subordinate explanatory/reason clause here ('for/because'). زان شب = 'that night' (زان = 'of/from that' used adverbially). نه آگاه بود is the negative past copula construction: نه negates the adjective آگاه 'aware/alert', and بود is the past 'was' — 'was not aware'.
segments.s705-g69: بدان here is an archaic contraction (به آن) meaning 'in/on those' introducing the locative 'in those tents'. در reinforces the locative 'inside'. ندیدند is the past negative construction (نَ + دیدند) meaning 'did not see'; کس 'anyone/no one' follows the negative verb — typical poetic order where the negated verb precedes its object.
segments.s705-g70: جز از introduces the exception 'except from/among'. ویژه یاران is a noun phrase 'the special/chosen companions'; بهرام specifies whose companions. و بس ('and that is enough' / 'and that is all') is a fixed poetic tag closing the clause and limiting the exception.
segments.s705-g71: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. بهرام is the subject, زان لشکر 'of/that army' indicates the object/topic of awareness, and آگاه گشت is a two-word predicate: آگاه 'aware' as adjective/participial and گشت (past of گشتن) 'became' — together 'became aware'. Word order is Subject + adverbial + predicate, common in epic style.
segments.s705-g72: بیامد is a simple past 'came'. بران (بر + آن) functions adverbially 'to those/thereupon' pointing to the tents; خیمه‌ها is the destination. برگذشت is a compound past (بر + گذشت) meaning 'passed by' or 'went past'. Subject (Bahram) is elided and understood from the previous clause.

sentences.s705-s41: They loaded everything, and he himself mounted; in the middle he blocked the way of return.
sentences.s705-s21: All are pure like a safeguard; they are like inner ear-jewels to the crown.
sentences.s705-s22: Then night also rose from the dark mountain; both companies of the army returned.
sentences.s705-s23: When the herdsmen and the bell came, there were not many asleep among the host.
sentences.s705-s24: The world-seeking bondman left from there; Tapt (he) strode between the two armies.
sentences.s705-s25: Kandauri, a melodious and eloquent herald, looked toward the army.
sentences.s705-s26: He ordered that Bargī sit up; by rousing them he closed the gap.
sentences.s705-s27: Thus, so that he might drive into the space between the two hosts and leave no numerous enemy remaining.
sentences.s705-s28: He raised a cry: 'O people — sinners and seekers of fortune!'
sentences.s705-s17: There is one host — this mud and the locusts — that has seized the wilderness, all the sand and shakh.
sentences.s705-s18: Neither was it noble to stand gaping and shed blood, nor for this king to cling to a servant.
sentences.s705-s19: Whoever among you wishes a warning from us — that warning is better than being killed or exhausted in battle.
sentences.s705-s20: Khosrow said to him: ‘Whoever is guilty of sin, turn aside and go; I am not one who seeks a grudge.’
sentences.s705-s9: The king's light hem came in, and he made an ill-omened sign to Bahram Chubin.
sentences.s705-s10: He struck a spear at his belt; his armor held — its fastening did not break.
sentences.s705-s11: The spear's tip was split in two; the man's heart went astray, filled with fear.
sentences.s705-s12: When the spear broke, the king was upset; he struck his sword at the maghfar, seeking vengeance.
sentences.s705-s29: Whoever among you is guiltier than he, let him be the more renowned in inward battle.
sentences.s705-s30: The king of the world entreated his God to pardon the sin that he had done, both manifest and hidden.
sentences.s705-s31: When a cry rose up on the dark nights, each set (or bent) his ear to the sound.
sentences.s705-s32: All the famed Bahramian leaders prepared to go, binding (or girding) their heads as they set out together.
sentences.s705-s13: Swords shattered everywhere; on that body the covering (mughaffar) settled.
sentences.s705-s14: Everyone who saw (it) cried “bravo,” and likewise the one who heard the clang of iron.
sentences.s705-s15: The illustrious ones went in from the rear; thus they smote that army and put it to disorder.
sentences.s705-s16: The bondman strode proudly up near the king and (cried), “O— your crown is loftier than the wheel of the heavens and the moon.”
sentences.s705-s1: Then an army came down from the mountain; the world was filled with the dust of the black horsemen.
sentences.s705-s2: Thereupon Bahram put his army in motion, and by day no light remained inside.
sentences.s705-s3: People kept saying that whoever drove an army needed wisdom, manliness, and organization.
sentences.s705-s4: The brave, when they saw my brick, fashioned me into that very heroism.
sentences.s705-s5: They chose me above the kings, that I might cast my sweet, flowing name upon the earth.
sentences.s705-s6: From the host, the king was struck with astonishment; he handled his bow and (took) an arrow‑shaft.
sentences.s705-s7: He struck suddenly at the king's waist; the arrow stuck, hanging in the way.
sentences.s705-s8: When a certain servant saw the arrow‑wound, he came out from the brocade and pulled it out.
sentences.s705-s37: He thus said to his companions: "Now flight will come — from calm, with an uprising."
sentences.s705-s38: He asked the caravan‑leaders for three thousand camels — foam‑spraying, renowned beasts.
sentences.s705-s39: From anything in the treasure that could be taken — from the spreadables and from the edibles.
sentences.s705-s40: Of (things) golden and silver, and of an ivory throne; likewise that yārah (ornament), a golden necklace and a crown.
sentences.s705-s33: When the bright summit of the world rose from the mountain, the day adorned the earth with carnage.
sentences.s705-s34: The whole plain was without men and the donkey‑stalls were empty, for Bahram that night was not aware.
sentences.s705-s35: In those tents no one was seen except Bahram’s chosen companions — and that was all.
sentences.s705-s36: When Bahram became aware of that army, he came to those tents and passed by them.
